abstract.text: A Swift engine for writing parser-driven interactive fiction — text adventures in the tradition of Zork and Infocom’s ZIL.

inlineContent.text: Gnusto turns a single Swift type into a playable text adventure. You
your world — its rooms, its things, and the rules that govern them — and the engine parses player input, runs the turn, and prints the result.

code: let intro = "You wake in a small, bright room."
description("A plain white room with a single door, to the north.")

A complete game: declarations, geography, and rules.
The geography and initial placements of a game, declared in one block:
Attach game logic to items, locations, and the world.
Give a region its own declarations — its own type, and even its own package.
The collected content bundles a game declares, in declaration order.
Alternative nouns the parser accepts for the item.
One statement in a
block: an exit, a blocked exit, an initial item
placement, or the player’s starting location.
The paragraph used to mention the item in a room description until the
player has touched it (ZIL’s FDESC).
Where the game’s text goes and player input comes from. Synchronous by
design: a blocking console read is fine for a CLI; an async variant can
be added later without breaking this protocol’s clients.
All problems with a game definition, reported at once.
A self-contained slice of a game’s world: its own rooms, items, and
state, plus the geography, rules, and verbs that go with them.
Carry your own data on entities and in globals, alongside the engine’s closed core.
The result builder for
trait blocks.
One piece of game logic: a phase, an owner, the intents it watches, and a
synchronous body that reads and writes the world through proxies.
The item is part of the scenery: it cannot be taken, and it is never
listed in room descriptions (its
text, if any, still appears).
One row of the verb table: a verb token sequence, the sentence shape it
accepts, and the intent it produces. Data, not code — games can add rows
through their
block to teach the parser new player-typeable verbs.
Ends the game. The engine prints the final score after the turn’s output.
The status line a handler can display: location, score, and turn count.

What happens between the player pressing Return and the next prompt.

Feeds a fixed list of commands and records everything into a transcript —
for tests and automated playthroughs. Ships in the library because game
authors want transcript tests too.
A Swift engine for writing parser-driven interactive fiction — text adventures in the tradition of Zork and Infocom’s ZIL.

Blocks the current action with a complaint. The default action and any
remaining
rules are skipped; world time still passes.
Additional words the parser accepts before the item’s noun.

The display name of an item. The last word becomes the item’s primary
noun; the leading words double as adjectives.

Whether the game is in progress or how it ended.
The text shown when the item is examined (or read).
